skill.name: distributed-debugging-debug-trace
skill.description: Configure debugging workflows, distributed tracing, diagnostic logging, profiling, and production-safe troubleshooting patterns for multi-service applications.
skill.summary: Plan and implement debug and trace practices for distributed systems.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis findings are mostly documentation snippets, but several copyable examples are confirmed high-risk if applied directly. The playbook includes unauthenticated remote debugging with Runtime.evaluate, telemetry that captures request bodies and identifiers, production debug response export, and innerHTML rendering from WebSocket data. No prompt injection attempt was found.

high_findings.title: Unauthenticated Remote Debug Execution Pattern
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The playbook opens the Node inspector on 0.0.0.0 and creates a WebSocket debug server that forwards client expressions to Runtime.evaluate. If copied without authentication and network controls, this enables remote code execution and runtime data access.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky semantics are explicit: an externally bound inspector receives WebSocket messages and evaluates supplied expressions. The code is presented as documentation, but the copied pattern is clearly dangerous.

high_findings.title: Sensitive Request Data Added to Telemetry
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The tracing examples record request bodies, user identifiers, and session identifiers as span attributes. Those values can contain secrets or personal data and may be exported to external observability systems.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The snippet directly serializes request.body and stores user and session identifiers in traces. The exporter endpoints show these traces can leave the process boundary.

high_findings.title: Production Debug Data Exposure
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The production debugging example enables debug mode from headers, captures console output, returns debug context in a response header, and can create heap snapshots in production. This risks leaking sensitive runtime state.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses a token and IP check, but still demonstrates returning collected debug context and creating runtime snapshots. Legitimate use is possible only with strict controls.

medium_findings.title: Dashboard XSS from WebSocket Data
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The debug dashboard inserts metrics, traces, and log fields into innerHTML after parsing WebSocket messages. Untrusted log messages or paths could execute script in the dashboard.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly assigns dynamic WebSocket payload fields to innerHTML. The impact depends on who can send dashboard data, so medium severity is appropriate.

medium_findings.title: Profiler and Heap Snapshot Files May Persist Sensitive Data
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The profiler examples write CPU profiles and heap snapshots to disk and expose development endpoints for manual collection. These artifacts often contain memory values, request data, and application structure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Writing profiler artifacts is normal for debugging, but the examples lack retention, permission, or redaction guidance. The routes are development-gated, reducing severity.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Network and Process Debug Tooling
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The Docker debugging setup installs gdb, strace, tcpdump, exposes inspector ports, and binds Node debugging to all interfaces. This is useful for diagnostics but expands the attack surface.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documentation, not package execution. They are still dangerous defaults if copied into a production image or exposed container.

low_findings.title: Several Static Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The detections in SKILL.md are Markdown resource references and descriptive prompt text, not executable code. Many environment variable hits are configuration examples, but the playbook still contains separate confirmed risks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited SKILL.md lines contain metadata and Markdown paths only. Configuration examples explain part of the static volume, but do not dismiss the confirmed high-risk snippets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Runtime.evaluate Exposed Through WebSocket
dangerous_patterns.description: The remote debug server parses WebSocket messages and passes command.expression to Runtime.evaluate. This is a direct code execution primitive if unauthenticated clients can connect.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from WebSocket message to Runtime.evaluate is direct and visible in the snippet.

dangerous_patterns.title: Inspector Bound to All Interfaces
dangerous_patterns.description: The debugging examples bind the inspector host to 0.0.0.0 and expose debugging ports. This can expose debug control outside the local machine.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The host and Docker debug settings explicitly use 0.0.0.0 for inspector access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Request Body Captured in Trace Span
dangerous_patterns.description: The OpenTelemetry hook serializes request.body into a span attribute. This can store credentials, tokens, or personal data in tracing backends.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact request body capture is explicit, and trace exporters are configured nearby.

dangerous_patterns.title: innerHTML Used with Dynamic Debug Data
dangerous_patterns.description: The dashboard renders parsed WebSocket metrics, traces, and logs with innerHTML. This can create XSS when log or trace fields are attacker-controlled.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Dynamic fields from WebSocket messages are interpolated into HTML strings and assigned to innerHTML.

content.value_statement: Distributed systems are hard to debug when logs, traces, and profiles are disconnected. This skill helps teams plan trace boundaries, debug workflows, and diagnostic standards.
actual_capabilities: Maps services, spans, correlation IDs, and trace boundaries for distributed applications.
Suggests local and remote debugging workflows for Node.js, TypeScript, React, and containerized services.
Provides OpenTelemetry, logging, profiling, memory monitoring, and dashboard implementation patterns.
Defines sampling, debug flags, log levels, and production diagnostic controls.
Helps create team playbooks for incident debugging and repeatable troubleshooting.
limitations: The playbook contains examples that need security hardening before production use.
It does not inspect a live system unless the user provides code, logs, or architecture details.
It cannot guarantee complete trace coverage without runtime validation.
Some examples are JavaScript focused and need adaptation for other stacks.

use_cases.title: Prepare Multi-Service Observability
use_cases.description: Design trace spans, log fields, and correlation IDs before adding instrumentation across services.

use_cases.title: Investigate Production Incidents
use_cases.description: Create a controlled debugging plan that captures useful diagnostics without exposing secrets.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Debugging
use_cases.description: Turn local debugging, profiling, and tracing practices into reusable team guidelines.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Debug Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new service.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a debugging plan for this service. Include local debugging, log fields, and trace boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Add Distributed Tracing
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this service architecture and suggest OpenTelemetry spans, attributes, sampling, and correlation IDs.

prompt_templates.title: Harden Production Debugging
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this production debugging approach. Identify data exposure risks and propose safer controls.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Incident Playbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an incident debugging playbook for these services, including trace queries, profile capture, rollback checks, and ownership.

output_examples.input: A team needs tracing for an API, worker, and database service.
output: A service map with span ownership and propagation points.
A recommended set of shared trace and log fields.
A rollout checklist for sampling, dashboards, and validation.

output_examples.input: An SRE wants production debugging without exposing secrets.
output: A risk-ranked debugging plan with access controls.
A redaction checklist for logs, traces, and snapshots.
A short incident workflow for capture, review, and cleanup.

output_examples.input: A developer wants to diagnose slow requests in a Node.js service.
output: A profiling plan with sampling limits and artifact handling.
Next steps for comparing baseline and degraded performance.

best_practices: Start with service boundaries, correlation IDs, and safe default sampling before adding detailed instrumentation.
Redact secrets, request bodies, session data, and personal data before exporting logs or traces.
Limit remote debugging to authenticated, temporary, network-restricted sessions with clear cleanup steps.
anti_patterns: Do not bind remote debuggers to public interfaces without strong authentication and network controls.
Do not store raw request bodies, tokens, or session identifiers in trace attributes.
Do not render logs or trace values with innerHTML in a browser dashboard.

faq.question: What systems does this skill fit best?
faq.answer: It fits distributed applications that need coordinated logs, traces, profiling, and production diagnostics.

faq.question: Can it generate implementation examples?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides patterns for debugging, tracing, logging, profiling, dashboards, and team playbooks.

faq.question: Is the playbook safe to copy directly?
faq.answer: No. Several examples need authentication, redaction, network restrictions, and safer rendering before production use.

faq.question: Does it replace an observability platform?
faq.answer: No. It helps design instrumentation and workflows that can feed tools such as tracing and logging platforms.

faq.question: Can it help during incidents?
faq.answer: Yes. It can structure trace review, debug capture, profiling, and follow-up documentation.

faq.question: What input should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide service names, request paths, current logs, tracing tools, runtime stack, and production safety constraints.
